course_info.title: Mobile and IoT Security
prerequisites: CY201 - Ethical Hacking and Penetration Testing

learning_objectives: Compare iOS and Android security architectures
Understand application sandboxing and permission models
readings: OWASP Mobile Security Testing Guide Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Mobile devices are the primary computing platform for billions of users. Their security architectures differ fundamentally from desktop systems, with unique strengths and weaknesses.
key_concepts: iOS security: Secure Enclave, App Store review, code signing, sandboxing
Android security: SELinux enforcement, permission model, Verified Boot
Application sandboxing: process isolation, data storage restrictions
Permission models: iOS vs Android runtime permissions
Mobile threat landscape: malicious apps, network attacks, physical access
lecture_notes.demonstration: Examining the iOS and Android security models side-by-side, demonstrating app sandboxing boundaries and permission enforcement

review.value: Each app runs in its own process with its own file system partition. Apps cannot access other apps' data without explicit permission. This is the foundation of mobile security.
iOS: closed ecosystem, mandatory code signing, App Store review. Android: open ecosystem, sideloading possible, Play Protect scanning. iOS has smaller attack surface; Android has more flexibility.

learning_objectives: Perform static and dynamic analysis of mobile applications
Use Frida for runtime manipulation of mobile apps

lecture_notes.introduction: Mobile application security testing combines traditional application security with mobile-specific techniques for analyzing apps running in sandboxed environments.
key_concepts: OWASP Mobile Top 10: Improper Credential Usage, Inadequate Supply Chain, Insecure Auth/Authorization
Static analysis: APK decompilation with jadx, IPA analysis with class-dump
Dynamic analysis: Frida hooking for runtime inspection
Network traffic interception: SSL pinning bypass
Insecure data storage: SharedPreferences, Keychain misuse
lecture_notes.demonstration: Decompiling an Android APK with jadx, finding hardcoded API keys, then using Frida to bypass SSL pinning and intercept API traffic
setup_requirements: Frida with frida-server on Android emulator
Burp Suite with mobile proxy configuration

review.value: Apps pin the expected server certificate to prevent MITM. Frida scripts hook the certificate validation function and force it to accept any certificate, enabling traffic interception for testing.

Storing sensitive data in SharedPreferences (Android) or UserDefaults (iOS) without encryption. Root/jailbreak gives access to all app data. Use Android Keystore or iOS Keychain with biometric protection.

learning_objectives: Map the IoT attack surface across device, network, and cloud layers
Identify common IoT vulnerabilities and exploitation techniques
Analyze IoT communication protocols for security weaknesses

lecture_notes.introduction: IoT devices extend the attack surface into the physical world. Their constrained resources, long deployment lifecycles, and often-poor security practices make them attractive targets.
key_concepts: IoT architecture: device, gateway, network, cloud, application layers
OWASP IoT Top 10: weak passwords, insecure network services, insecure ecosystem interfaces
IoT protocols: MQTT, CoAP, BLE, Zigbee, Z-Wave
Firmware extraction and analysis
Physical attacks: JTAG, UART, SPI flash dumping
lecture_notes.demonstration: Extracting firmware from a consumer IoT device via SPI flash, analyzing it with binwalk, and finding hardcoded credentials
setup_requirements: Bus Pirate or logic analyzer for hardware interfaces

review.value: MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport) is widely used in IoT. Default configurations often lack authentication, allow wildcard topic subscriptions, and transmit data unencrypted. TLS and ACLs are essential.
binwalk extracts filesystem from firmware images. Look for: hardcoded credentials, private keys, API tokens, debug interfaces, and known vulnerable library versions.

class_schedule.topic: IoT Network Security and Protocol Analysis
learning_objectives: Analyze MQTT, CoAP, and BLE protocols for security vulnerabilities
Implement network-level protections for IoT deployments
Detect compromised IoT devices on a network
readings: IoT Security: Architecture and Solutions Ch. 5-7

lecture_notes.introduction: IoT protocols were designed for constrained environments, often sacrificing security for efficiency. Understanding their vulnerabilities is critical for securing IoT deployments.
key_concepts: MQTT security: authentication, ACLs, TLS configuration
BLE security: pairing modes, MITM attacks, sniffing with Ubertooth
CoAP security: DTLS for transport security
IoT network segmentation: dedicated VLANs, firewall policies
Anomaly detection for IoT device behavior
lecture_notes.demonstration: Intercepting MQTT traffic on an unencrypted broker, subscribing to all topics to observe data exposure, then securing the broker with TLS and ACLs

review.value: Bluetooth Low Energy 'Just Works' pairing mode provides no MITM protection. An attacker with an Ubertooth can intercept pairing and decrypt all subsequent communication. Use 'Numeric Comparison' or 'Passkey Entry' modes.
IoT devices should be on a dedicated VLAN with strict firewall rules: allow only required protocols to specific cloud endpoints. Block all lateral communication to corporate network.

class_schedule.topic: IoT Firmware Security and Secure Boot
learning_objectives: Implement secure boot for IoT devices
Protect IoT devices against physical tampering

lecture_notes.introduction: IoT devices operate in physically accessible environments. Hardware security features and secure boot chains protect device integrity from manufacturing through end-of-life.
key_concepts: Secure boot chain: ROM bootloader to verified OS
Hardware root of trust: TPM, Secure Enclave, TrustZone
Secure OTA firmware updates: signed images, rollback protection
Physical security: tamper detection, secure element, fuse-based key storage
Device lifecycle: provisioning, operation, decommissioning
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implementing a secure boot chain on a Raspberry Pi with verified firmware updates and demonstrating how unsigned firmware is rejected
setup_requirements: Raspberry Pi with secure boot configured

review.value: Each stage of the boot process verifies the signature of the next stage before executing it. The chain starts from an immutable ROM bootloader that trusts only a specific public key.
Firmware updates must be signed by the manufacturer's private key, encrypted in transit, verified before installation, and support rollback protection to prevent downgrade attacks.

class_schedule.topic: Mobile Device Management and IoT Fleet Security
learning_objectives: Implement MDM policies for enterprise mobile deployments
Design security monitoring for large IoT fleets
Apply NIST guidelines for IoT device management

lecture_notes.introduction: Securing individual devices is necessary but insufficient. Enterprise mobile and IoT deployments require fleet-level management, monitoring, and policy enforcement.
key_concepts: MDM capabilities: device enrollment, policy enforcement, remote wipe
BYOD security: containerization, work profiles, conditional access
IoT fleet management: device inventory, certificate rotation, monitoring
Zero trust for IoT: device identity, mutual TLS, microsegmentation
Regulatory considerations: GDPR for IoT data, medical device regulations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configuring MDM policies for an enterprise mobile fleet: mandatory encryption, VPN requirement, application whitelist, and remote wipe capability
setup_requirements: MDM platform (Microsoft Intune trial or similar)

review.value: MDM can remotely erase a lost or stolen device. Selective wipe removes only corporate data (containerized). Full wipe resets the entire device. Policy should define when each is used.

IoT devices use certificates for authentication. Certificates expire and must be rotated without manual intervention. Automated certificate management protocols (EST, ACME) enable fleet-scale rotation.

quizzes.topic: Mobile Security and IoT Attack Surface

questions.question: An Android application stores the user's OAuth token in SharedPreferences without encryption. On a rooted device, an attacker runs 'cat /data/data/com.example.app/shared_prefs/auth.xml' and obtains the token. Explain the vulnerability, the risk, and two specific remediation approaches.
questions.answer: Vulnerability: SharedPreferences stores data as plaintext XML files in the app's private directory. While the Android sandbox prevents other apps from reading this on non-rooted devices, root access bypasses all sandbox restrictions. The OAuth token grants access to the user's account. Remediation 1: Use Android Keystore to store the token. The Keystore backs storage with hardware security (TEE/StrongBox), making extraction resistant even on rooted devices. Bind key access to biometric authentication for additional protection. Remediation 2: Use EncryptedSharedPreferences (Jetpack Security library) which transparently encrypts both keys and values using AES-256-GCM with keys stored in the Android Keystore.

questions.question: You discover an IoT smart thermostat that communicates with its cloud service over unencrypted MQTT (port 1883) and uses a default username 'admin' with password 'admin'. Describe the attack scenario this enables and three specific security controls the manufacturer should implement.
questions.answer: Attack scenario: An attacker on the same network can connect to the MQTT broker with default credentials, subscribe to the thermostat's topics to monitor occupancy patterns (home/away), and publish commands to control the thermostat (e.g., set heating to maximum while the user is away, causing damage or high energy bills). Controls: (1) Mandatory TLS on MQTT (port 8883) with mutual certificate authentication, eliminating plaintext traffic and password-based auth. (2) Unique per-device credentials provisioned during manufacturing with no default passwords. The device's private key should be stored in a secure element. (3) MQTT ACLs restricting each device to publish/subscribe only to its own topics (e.g., device/{serial}/telemetry), preventing one compromised device from affecting others.

quizzes.topic: IoT Protocols, Firmware Security, and Fleet Management

questions.question: An IoT camera manufacturer pushes firmware updates over HTTP without digital signatures. Explain two specific attacks this enables and design a secure firmware update mechanism.
questions.answer: Attack 1: Man-in-the-middle firmware replacement. An attacker intercepts the HTTP update and replaces it with a malicious firmware containing a backdoor. Every camera that updates receives the attacker's firmware. Attack 2: Rollback attack. An attacker replays an older firmware version with known vulnerabilities, even after the manufacturer patched them. Secure update mechanism: (1) HTTPS for transport with certificate pinning to the manufacturer's server. (2) Firmware images signed with the manufacturer's Ed25519 private key. The device verifies the signature against the embedded public key before installation. (3) Version number stored in secure (fused) storage; the device rejects firmware with a version number lower than current (anti-rollback). (4) A/B partition scheme so the device can roll back to the previous good firmware if the new one fails to boot.

rubric.key: Two valid attacks from unsigned HTTP updates

questions.question: A hospital deploys 500 IoT medical devices across its network. Describe a zero-trust architecture for managing these devices, addressing identity, network access, and monitoring.
questions.answer: Identity: Each device has a unique X.509 certificate issued during manufacturing and enrolled in the hospital's PKI. Device identity is verified via mutual TLS for every connection. Certificates are automatically rotated via EST protocol before expiration. Network access: Devices are placed on a dedicated IoT VLAN with microsegmentation. Each device type has a specific firewall policy allowing only required communication (e.g., patient monitors can only send data to the monitoring server on port 5683/CoAP). No device-to-device communication is allowed. 802.1X enforces network admission. Monitoring: Deploy a specialized IoT security platform (e.g., Armis, Medigate) that builds behavioral baselines per device type and alerts on anomalies. Monitor for: unexpected outbound connections, firmware version compliance, certificate expiration warnings, and vulnerability advisories affecting deployed device versions.

major_assignments.title: Mobile and IoT Security Assessment
major_assignments.description: Perform a comprehensive security assessment of a provided mobile application and IoT device. Produce a professional security assessment report with findings and remediation recommendations.
objectives: Test a mobile application against OWASP Mobile Top 10
Assess an IoT device across all attack surface layers
Produce a unified security assessment report

criteria: Device, network, and cloud layers assessed
Each finding: description, evidence, impact, remediation
Both quick wins and strategic improvements
Thorough assessment across mobile and IoT attack surfaces
Professional findings with evidence and impact analysis
Report quality suitable for vendor submission

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of mobile and IoT security concepts

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on mobile app testing and IoT security labs

major_assignments.description: Mobile and IoT security assessment project

midterm_examination.description: Assessment covering mobile security and IoT attack surface

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all mobile and IoT security topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in a grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be completed in pairs. All reports must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Mobile app testing must use provided test applications only. IoT testing must use provided lab devices. Testing personal or third-party devices is prohibited.
course_policies.lab_safety: Handle IoT lab devices carefully. Do not attempt physical attacks that may permanently damage shared equipment without instructor approval.
